front: Poem: the speaker tells the addressee 'the jewel you lost was blue' in this poem from Birthday Letters
Poem: 'The Blue Flannel Suit' describes clothes worn by the addressee on the day she began teaching at Smith College, from this collection
Poem: the speaker says 'When you wanted bees I never dreamed / It meant your Daddy had come up out of the well' in this Birthday Letters poem
Poem: the speaker wrote 'the dreamer in her had fallen in love with me' about Assia Wevill in this collection
Author: Seamus Heaney wrote a poem titled 'On First Looking Into' this poet's collection Birthday Letters after this poet's death
Poem: a hawk's 'wings hold all creation in a weightless quiet' and it 'hangs still' at the 'master-fulcrum of violence'
Poem: at the end, 'the ponderous shires crash on' the title creature and 'mix his heart's blood with the mire of the land'
Poem: the title animal sets 'neat prints into the snow' and enters 'the dark hole of the head,' analogizing the creative process
Poem: describes a 'sudden sharp hot stink' of the title animal entering the poet's mind
Poem: a hawk declares 'I kill where I please because it is all mine' and 'It took the whole of Creation to produce my foot... Now I hold Creation in my foot'
Poem: God tries to teach the title character to say 'love,' but he instead produces 'Man's bodiless prodigious head' and 'woman's vulva'
Poem: the title character responds to 'who is stronger than death?' with 'me, evidently'
back: "Examination at the Womb-Door" from Crow (Ted Hughes)

front: Poem: 'Lineage' tells a creation myth replacing God with screaming, which 'begat Blood / Who begat Eye / Who begat Fear / Who begat Wing'
Poem: the title figure 'flies the black flag of himself' after 'nailing heaven and earth together'
Poem: this collection was illustrated by Leonard Baskin and its title figure journeys through the universe in search of his female creator
Author: became Poet Laureate after Philip Larkin refused the title following John Betjeman's death
Author: made major changes to Plath's Ariel, including moving the bee poems to the middle of the collection
Poem: describes otters as 'Four-legged yet water gifted, to outfish fish'
Author: collaborated with Seamus Heaney on the poetry anthology The Rattle Bag
Author: translated Frank Wedekind's Spring Awakening from German in the 1970s
Poem: the title man 'survives among hills' while 'the dust of Achilles and Cuchulain itches'
Author: Elizabeth Costello uses this poet's 'The Jaguar' (about an animal whose 'stride is wilderness of freedom') to argue poets can embody animals
